petitioner_background: The petitioner is a painter (artist) who trained in his native country before relocating to the United States. He sought classification as an individual of extraordinary ability under EB-1A (INA § 203(b)(1)(A)). The Nebraska Service Center Director denied the petition, finding he did not satisfy at least three of ten initial evidentiary criteria, and also concluded he had not shown his entry would substantially benefit the United States. The AAO dismissed his appeal, and the matter is now before the AAO on a motion to reopen.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of an 'honorable mention award' from an Oil Painters of America (OPA) Online Showcase, including a letter from OPA congratulating him on the 'wonderful achievement' of 'winning an Honorable Mention Award.' He also submitted evidence of honorable mentions at two Art Contests (name redacted), photographs from the award ceremony showing adult participants, an article noting the contest was open to participants ages 15 and up, information about other individuals who won prizes at the contests, materials about the effect of political upheaval on the art community, and the 'Rules of the [redacted] Art Prize' to support the assertion that 'Many recognized art competitions in the world allow open entries.' He did not claim to have won the [redacted] Art Prize itself.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO concluded the petitioner had not shown the OPA honorable mention is nationally or internationally recognized, noting that OPA's characterization of its own awards is not evidence of recognition beyond the awarding organization. Private correspondence from the awarding entity does not demonstrate national or international recognition. The AAO rejected the petitioner's argument that a prestigious organization must give prestigious awards, explaining that the regulation requires recognition of the awards themselves, not the reputation of the awarding organization, and different awards from the same organization might not enjoy the same level of recognition. Evidence about higher-level OPA prizes does not establish recognition of honorable mentions. Regarding the two Art Contests, the petitioner submitted no new evidence directly addressing whether his honorable mentions are nationally or internationally recognized, and the rules of a different competition he did not enter are not comparable. The AAO noted that while press coverage is not required to prove he won the mentions, it is relevant to show whether they attracted the attention expected of a widely recognized prize. The AAO found this criterion not met.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner previously claimed membership in two associations (names redacted). On motion, he submitted: (1) a copy of an email from the association dated August 16, 2019, welcoming him as a member (at the lowest of three tiers); (2) a printout from the organization's website stating 'The first criteria [sic] is talent, regardless of the length of the CV, years of experience, awards and honors received.... All our academicians have been subject to a rigorous selection and selected for excellence in their work'; and (3) a new letter from the organization's president repeating assertions from prior letters and stating that jurors consider 'pictorial techniques,' 'compositional ability,' 'inherent meaning' and 'uniqueness' of submitted works. The printout also indicated the lowest tier must show 'competence and understanding.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the welcome email dated August 16, 2019 was more than six weeks after the July 2, 2019 petition filing date and five weeks after the July 11, 2019 RFE, so it cannot establish eligibility at the time of filing as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(1). The evidence was consistent with the prior conclusion that participation in competitions in 2016 and 2017 was not evidence of membership. Furthermore, the petitioner's tier was the lowest of three tiers of membership, requiring only 'competence and understanding' rather than higher-level criteria. The AAO noted grammatical and spelling errors in the web printout ('criteria' instead of 'criterion,' 'authot' instead of 'author,' 'justificative' instead of 'justification'). The factors listed by the president (pictorial techniques, compositional ability, inherent meaning, uniqueness) relate to subjective evaluations of an artist's skills but do not rise to the level of outstanding achievements. The petitioner still did not submit organizational documentation such as bylaws or a constitution, and in the absence of documented, enforceable rules and definition of key terms, the information was too vague to establish that membership meets regulatory requirements.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted: (1) printouts from the website of an online gallery listing prices for his work and other paintings of the same size, style, and media (all for paintings still available for sale); (2) an exhibition catalog from 2018 in which his work was not listed, but which the petitioner stated contained mostly lower prices than his work; (3) invoices showing three paintings sold for $5,000-$6,000 each in 2014-2015 and five others sold for between $9,010 and $11,200 in 2019-2020 (two invoices dated after the filing date, one reflecting a 20% discount from asking price); and (4) a comparison of his 2014-2015 prices with his home country's monthly minimum wage in the same month and year. Total documented sales were $68,168 over five and a half years, with an average price of $7,643 for the six invoices dated before the July 2019 filing date.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the online gallery listings were for paintings still available for sale and therefore do not show prices buyers actually paid, and the petitioner did not establish that one gallery is representative of the field as a whole. The 2018 exhibition catalog prices were not shown to be representative of the field overall, and his work was not even listed. The invoices were insufficient to establish any definite trend because there were only a few, with a multi-year gap between 2015 and 2019, and recent invoices were for much heavier (and presumably larger) works. The 20% discount on one invoice demonstrated that purchasers do not always pay full published prices. The comparison to the home country's minimum wage was 'not instructive,' because regulations require remuneration to be significantly high in relation to others in the field, not in relation to local minimum wage or the general population. The petitioner did not establish that his figures were significantly high in relation to others in the field.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence of the petitioner's display at artistic exhibitions or showcases (specific evidence not detailed in this motion decision, as this criterion was already found satisfied at the appellate stage).
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director concluded, and the AAO agreed, that the petitioner meets criterion (vii) relating to display at artistic exhibitions or showcases. This was not contested on motion.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO granted the motion to reopen in part because the petitioner submitted relevant new evidence, but dismissed the appeal because the new evidence did not establish eligibility or overcome the grounds for dismissal. The petitioner only established one criterion (exhibitions/display), falling short of the three-criterion minimum required under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3). The AAO did not reach a final merits determination because the petitioner failed at the initial evidentiary threshold. The AAO also noted that references to previously submitted evidence fall outside the scope of a motion to reopen, which is limited to new facts and supporting evidence; arguments that the prior decision was incorrect on the original record would belong in a motion to reconsider, which the petitioner did not file. The criteria numbered (iii) published material and (viii) leading role were deemed abandoned because the petitioner did not contest the prior conclusions on those.
legal_citations: Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011)
Sepulveda v. U.S. Att'y Gen., 401 F.3d 1226 (11th Cir. 2005)
United States v. Cunningham, 161 F.3d 1343 (11th Cir. 1998)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Several procedural issues: (1) The welcome email establishing membership was dated August 16, 2019, after the July 2, 2019 petition filing date, violating the requirement under 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(1) that eligibility be established at the time of filing. (2) Two of the submitted sales invoices were also dated after the petition filing date. (3) The petitioner improperly attempted to rely on previously submitted evidence, which falls outside the scope of a motion to reopen (limited to new facts). (4) The criteria for published material (iii) and leading role (viii) were deemed abandoned/waived because the petitioner did not contest the prior adverse conclusions. (5) The AAO noted multiple grammatical and spelling errors in the organization's website printout ('criteria' for 'criterion,' 'authot' for 'author,' 'justificative' for 'justification'), undermining its reliability. No fraud or misrepresentation findings were made.
